  • Tutorial: Soil Types & Ground Temperature
  • Tutorial: Soil Types & Ground Temperature

Tutorial: Soil Types & Ground Temperature

GreenhousesJL includes a library of soil types with moisture-dependent thermal properties, and the Kusuda model for undisturbed ground temperature at depth.

Soil type presets

list_soil_types()
Presetλ_eff [W/(m·K)]ρcp [MJ/(m³·K)]MoistureUse case
Sand()1.061.7010%Well-drained, fast response
SandyLoam()1.292.0820%Common agricultural
Loam()1.182.2425%Default — balanced
ClayLoam()1.092.4430%Higher water retention
Clay()0.962.6335%Slow drainage, high capacity
Peat()0.422.8550%Very insulating, organic
Gravel()0.811.565%Drainage layer
ConcreteSlab()1.702.112%Greenhouse floor

Usage

# Use a specific soil type
p = GreenhouseParams(tmy; A=200.0, soil_type=Clay())

# Sandy soil with custom moisture
p = GreenhouseParams(tmy; A=200.0, soil_type=Sand(moisture=0.25))

# Set deep boundary temperature (Kelvin)
p = GreenhouseParams(tmy; A=200.0, soil_type=Loam(), T_deep=285.15)  # 12°C

Moisture-dependent properties

Thermal conductivity depends strongly on soil moisture (Johansen 1975):

soil = Loam()
λ_dry = effective_conductivity(soil; moisture=0.0)    # 0.25 W/(m·K)
λ_moist = effective_conductivity(soil; moisture=0.25)  # 1.18 W/(m·K)
λ_sat = effective_conductivity(soil; moisture=0.45)    # 1.50 W/(m·K)

Wet soil conducts 5× more than dry soil, this matters for greenhouse heating.

Kusuda ground temperature model

The undisturbed ground temperature at depth varies annually (Kusuda & Achenbach 1965):

\[T(z, t) = T_{mean} - T_{amp} \cdot e^{-z\sqrt{\pi/(365 \alpha)}} \cdot \cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - t_0 - \frac{z}{2}\sqrt{\frac{365}{\pi\alpha}})\right)\]

# Temperature at 2m depth in January (continental climate)
T = kusuda_temperature(2.0, 15; T_mean=12.0, T_amp=12.0)
# → 10.4°C
DepthJanuaryJulyAnnual swing
0.5 m3.9°C20.3°C16.4°C
1.0 m6.6°C17.6°C11.0°C
2.0 m10.4°C13.8°C3.4°C
5.0 m13.1°C10.9°C~0°C

At 2m depth, annual oscillation is only 3.4°C, the default T_deep is a reasonable approximation.

Impact on greenhouse heating

Simulation: 200m² greenhouse, 7 days winter, SP=18°C:

Soilλ_effE_heatvs Loam
Peat0.421434 kWh−7% (most insulating)
Gravel0.811445−7%
Sand1.061493−4%
Clay0.961541−1%
Loam1.181549ref

Peat soil saves ~8% heating vs loam because of its very low conductivity. The effect compounds over a full season.

References

  • de Vries, D.A. (1963). Thermal properties of soils. In: Physics of Plant Environment.
  • Johansen, O. (1975). Thermal conductivity of soils. PhD thesis, Trondheim.
  • Kusuda, T. & Achenbach, P.R. (1965). Earth temperature and thermal diffusivity at selected stations. ASHRAE Transactions, 71(1), 61–75.
